properties.mechanism: The heatmap cell value (Opens + Clicks grouped by day and hour) is built directly from the same hourly opens and clicks that the Engagement Timeline counts.
Campaign open and click rates are computed from the opens and clicks that the Engagement Timeline counts per hour.
The open rate and CTOR trends are calculated from the opened and clicked counts that the Engagement Timeline tracks.
A higher volume of emails sent produces more recipients who can open and click, driving up the opens and clicks counted in the timeline.
Emails that bounce or are flagged as spam never reach the inbox and so are never opened or clicked, so deliverability gates the opens and clicks the timeline records.
The Historical Comparison computes the same campaign-level Open Rate, Click Rate and CTOR (Opens/Clicks over Sends) that Campaign Performance produces, then compares them across time periods.
Both track the same email Open Rate and CTOR over time, so their period-over-period values move together.
Both compute Bounce Rate as Bounces/Sends on the same campaign sends, so the subject's bounce-rate component co-moves with Deliverability Health's bounce metric.
Segment Performance is the per-segment slice of the same campaign open/click rate metrics, so the segment values reliably co-move with the overall campaign-level rates they aggregate into.
Deliverability and bounce/spam rates determine how many emails are actually Delivered and reach the inbox, which directly drives the open and click rates measured per segment.
Both track the same Klaviyo open rate and click/CTOR engagement signals, so segment-level rates move together with the overall engagement rate trend over time.
This metric supplies Total Recipients summed per Trigger Type, which is exactly the denominator of the subject's Avg Open/Click Rate per Trigger Type formula.
Both compute Unique Opens/Clicks over Recipients for Klaviyo flows, so per-flow open/click rates co-move with the same rates aggregated by trigger type.
Flow Performance Trend uses the identical Unique Opens/Clicks over Recipients calculation tracked over time, so it reliably co-moves with engagement measured by trigger type.
Poor deliverability (high bounces/spam) means flow emails never reach the inbox, suppressing the unique opens and clicks that drive the subject's engagement rates.
The Performance Trend table computes the identical Open Rate and Click Rate (Unique Opens/Clicks over Recipients) for flows over time, so it co-moves directly with the Flow Performance Table.

Engagement by Flow Type aggregates the same Unique Opens/Clicks over Recipients rates per trigger type, so it reliably co-moves with the overall Flow Performance Table built from the same flow events.
Both tables measure flow engagement (open and click behavior) on the same Klaviyo flow sends, so their rates move together even though one uses Recipients and the other uses Sends/CTOR.
Poor deliverability (high bounce and spam rates reducing inbox placement) lowers how many flow emails reach inboxes, directly depressing the open and click rates in the Flow Performance Table.

Recipients is the denominator of the subject's Open Rate and Click Rate, and this metric supplies the underlying Unique Recipient counts (aggregated by trigger type) that the Flow Performance Table divides by.
Both compute flow Open Rate and Click Rate as Unique Opens/Clicks divided by Recipients on the same Klaviyo flow data, so they move together.
Engagement by Flow Type aggregates the same Unique Opens/Clicks over Recipients rates by trigger type, so it co-moves with the overall flow performance trend.
Both measure flow-level open and click engagement on Klaviyo flows, so their rates reliably co-move.
Total Recipients is the denominator of both the subject's Open Rate and Click Rate formulas.
Poor deliverability (bounces and spam complaints) keeps emails out of the inbox, which lowers the unique opens and clicks that drive the flow's open and click rates.
The subject's Total Recipients is computed by summing the per-flow unique recipient counts reported in the Flow Performance Table, then grouping them by trigger type.
Both metrics are derived from the same per-flow unique recipient counts, so a flow's recipient volume co-moves with its trigger-type recipient total.

Both metrics are computed from the same underlying unique-recipients-per-flow counts (one ranks the top flows by recipients, the other sums those same recipient counts by trigger type), so they co-move as flow send volume shifts.
The flow recipient counts that drive the subject's recipient share are the same per-flow recipient denominators used in this flow performance table, so the two metrics share inputs and co-move.
Click Rate Trend tracks the same SMS Click Rate = (Clicked / Sent) over time, so it co-moves directly with the subject campaign Click Rate.
Channel Comparison computes per-channel Click Rate = (Clicks / Sends), and its SMS-channel slice reflects the same SMS campaign engagement the subject measures.
SMS Flow Performance uses the identical Click Rate and Conversion Rate formulas on the same SMS audience, so campaign and flow engagement co-move with shared list and channel health.
Growing the SMS subscriber list expands the reachable, more recently opted-in audience, which raises Sent volume and tends to lift campaign click and conversion rates.
A healthier SMS list (higher subscription rate, cleaner composition) delivers messages to more engaged, valid subscribers, improving campaign click and conversion performance.
The SMS channel's click rate (Clicked SMS / Sent SMS) is one of the per-channel click rates that the cross-channel Channel Comparison aggregates.
The email channel's click rate (Clicks / Sends) is the email-side per-channel click rate that the Channel Comparison places against the SMS channel.
SMS Campaign click rate co-moves with the SMS channel's click rate feeding the comparison since both measure clicks per send on the same channel.
SMS campaign click rate uses the identical Clicks/Sent formula, so the campaign-level metric and the overall click-rate trend reliably co-move.
SMS flow click rate uses the same Clicks/Sent formula, so flow click performance moves together with the aggregate SMS click-rate trend.
The SMS slice of the per-channel click rate is computed the same way as this metric, so the two track each other over time.
A healthier SMS list with a higher proportion of actively subscribed, engaged recipients tends to raise the share of sent messages that get clicked.
Both compute SMS Click Rate as Clicks/Sent on the same Klaviyo SMS channel, so the flow click rate moves with the overall SMS click-rate time series.
SMS campaigns and SMS flows share the same audience, formulas (Click/Conversion Rate per Sent) and content style on the Klaviyo SMS channel, so their engagement rates co-move.
The SMS channel's click rate in the channel comparison is driven by the same SMS sends that include flows, so the two click-rate measures move together.
A healthier SMS list with a higher share of actively subscribed, valid numbers raises the click and conversion rates of SMS flows sent to that list.
Both metrics are driven by the same SMS subscription and unsubscribe events, so net list growth and list-health composition (subscribed vs unsubscribed share) reliably co-move.
Sending SMS campaigns prompts a portion of recipients to opt out, increasing unsubscribes and thereby lowering the subject's net list growth.
SMS flow messages (e.g. welcome and abandonment series) generate opt-outs that add to unsubscribes and reduce net list growth.
Net new subscribers and unsubscribes from list growth directly change the Subscribed and Unsubscribed counts that define SMS list composition and subscription rate.
Sending SMS campaigns triggers opt-outs (STOP replies) that increase the Unsubscribed count and erode the subscribed share of the list.

Both metrics compute SMS click rate as clicked SMS divided by sent SMS; the heatmap slices it by hour-of-day while the trend slices it by date, so they track the same underlying engagement signal and co-move.
Scheduling SMS campaign sends during the heatmap's best-performing hours lifts the campaign's click and conversion rates because recipients are more likely to be active and engaged at those times.
Applying the optimal send-hour insight to time-triggered SMS flow messages raises flow click and conversion rates by delivering messages when recipients are most responsive.
SMS Click Rate Trend is computed as Clicked SMS / Sent SMS, the exact two event counts the subject's Weekly Clicks and Weekly Sent track.
SMS campaign sends and clicks are a major component of the total weekly Sent/Clicked SMS event volume, so the two move together.
SMS flow sends and clicks feed into the same total weekly Sent/Clicked SMS event counts, so they co-move with the subject's trend.
The SMS channel's clicks and sends in the channel comparison are the same event stream the subject counts, so they statistically co-move.
Growth in the SMS subscriber list increases the addressable audience, driving up the weekly count of Sent SMS events.
The ranking sorts SMS messages by Click Rate, the per-message Click Rate = (Clicks / Sent) that SMS Campaign Performance computes for each message.
The sort key is Click Rate = (Clicked SMS Events / Sent SMS Events) x 100, which this metric defines as the input the ranking is built from.
SMS flow messages share the same Click Rate measure used to rank top messages, so high-performing flow and campaign messages tend to co-move on the leaderboard.
Higher bounce and spam rates degrade inbox placement, so fewer emails reach recipients and open/click rates fall.
Historical Comparison tracks the identical Open Rate, Click Rate, and CTOR definitions over prior periods, so the two co-move.
Engagement Rate Trends plots the same Open Rate and CTOR metrics over time, moving in lockstep with current campaign performance.
Content Type Performance computes the same open/click rates grouped by content category, so it co-moves with overall campaign performance.
Rising send volume increases subscriber fatigue, which tends to depress open and click rates.
The climate-zone composition of the subscriber base determines which geographic segments exist and how large they are, shaping the per-segment open/click rates reported in Segment Performance.
Both measure the same underlying email open/click engagement on Klaviyo email, so content-type open/click rates co-move with overall campaign open/click rates.
Engagement rate trends track the same open and click rates over time that content-type performance aggregates by category, so the two reliably co-move.
Poor deliverability (bounces/spam) reduces inbox placement, lowering the opens and clicks that drive content-type open and click rates.
Higher email send volume drives subscriber fatigue that depresses open and click rates across content categories.
Deliverability health gates how many sends actually reach recipients, which directly moves the open and CTOR engagement-rate trends.
Higher email send volume increases the risk of bounces and spam complaints, degrading the deliverability score.
Both metrics compute Bounce Rate from Bounces over Sends, so their bounce components move together over time.
Higher email send volume over time induces subscriber fatigue, which depresses open and click-to-open rates.
A higher volume of emails sent in a given day/hour produces more raw opens and clicks, raising the heatmap's engagement cell values.
Poor deliverability (bounces/spam filtering) reduces how many emails reach inboxes, lowering the opens and clicks that feed the heatmap.
Campaign Performance uses the identical Open Rate, Click Rate, and CTOR formulas on the same Klaviyo email events, so it co-moves with the aggregate engagement rate trend.
Historical Comparison tracks the same open-rate and CTOR engagement metrics across time periods, so it reliably co-moves with the engagement rate trend.
